oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Does source farm group size affect the performance and behavior of beef calves when commingled at a commercial feedlot?
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Brezina, C.M.
Dewell, G.A.
Millman, S.T.
Dewell, R.D.
Parsons, R.L.
Johnson, A.K.
Haley, D.B.
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Commingling calves from various sources into a single pen is a common practice in the feedlot industry and represents one of the many stressors calves face after leaving their home farm. While current research has focused on whether com­mingling causes distress, there is growing interest in under­standing how the social dynamics within a commingled group may influence calves. Our previous research has shown that the short-term performance of commingled calves that share no common-source farm with any pen mates (solitary calves) were more negatively impacted by castration stress compared to commingled calves housed with familiar pen mates from the same home farm. Building on these findings, the objective of this study was to determine if solitary calves’ long-term perfor­mance and behavior differed from that of familiar calves in a commercial feedlot and if the group size from their home farm, within a pen, affected these outcomes.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; Vol. 58 No. 2 (2025): 2025 AABP Annual Conference Proceedings; 257
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
